questions.q: Where should "System Rules" always be placed in a bot's personality?
questions.explain: The guide is explicit: System Rules should always go last, after identity, character profile, and any world/tracker info.

questions.q: In a Character Profile, which trio should be placed at the very top as "most important"?
questions.explain: Name, age, and physical description are flagged as "most important" and belong before personality description and everything else.

questions.q: True or False: it's best to refer to your bot as "{{char}}" constantly instead of its real name.
questions.explain: The guide says using the actual name is typically best — overusing {{char}} can confuse the bot.

questions.q: True or False: the NSFW / Sensitive Content Handling section is required in every bot template.
questions.explain: It's explicitly labeled OPTIONAL in the advanced template.

questions.q: Per the "Boundaries & Consistency Rules" example, what should a bot NEVER do?
options: Mention being an AI or language model
questions.explain: The "Never" list starts with: mention being an AI or language model, and break immersion.

questions.q: Which of these is NOT one of the three multi-bot methods in the guide?
questions.explain: "Auto-Translate Mode" isn't a real method here — the three are precoding, random NPC generation, and IP/franchise universes.

questions.q: True or False: precoding means writing out each character's full description individually (e.g. {{char1}}, {{char2}}...).
questions.explain: That's exactly the precoding approach shown in the Killer Derby example.

questions.q: What's the main practical benefit of saving a bot as a JSON file?
options: It makes the bot's writing smarter
It lets you upload the bot across sites without retyping everything
It's required by every chatbot site
questions.explain: JSON is a portable save file — it saves you from re-entering the whole character on every platform.

questions.q: True or False: every AI chatbot site supports uploading .json files.
questions.explain: The guide notes "many, but not all sites allow you to upload as .json files."

questions.q: In basic prompting, which part of the prompt tends to carry the most weight?
questions.explain: Words at the beginning of the prompt hold more weight than those at the end.

questions.q: Why do vague prompts often default toward photorealism?
options: Most training image-text pairs are photos/stock images
questions.explain: Since models learn from frequency, and most training pairs are photographic, "realistic" becomes the default baseline for vague prompts.

questions.q: Structured prompting relies mainly on:
questions.explain: It's built on attribute/keyword lists rather than full sentences.

questions.q: What's a downside of "grouping" prompt elements (art style: X + subject: Y) vs. stating them directly?
questions.explain: Grouping helps avoid value mix-ups but costs more character count than direct statement.

questions.q: True or False: you should type words like "not" or "no" inside the negative prompt field.
questions.explain: That creates a double negative — the field already means "avoid this," so adding negation flips the meaning.

questions.q: Negative prompts are good for fixing all of these EXCEPT:
options: Making the AI more creative overall
questions.explain: Too many negatives actually limit the AI's creativity — they're a filter, not a creativity boost.

questions.q: What's the universally recommended weight scale range to stay within?
questions.explain: Weights technically go 0.01–100, but the recommended safe range is 0.5–1.5 to avoid deformity.

questions.q: Which brackets should you use for factor weighting like (term:factor)?
questions.explain: Parentheses — the guide notes [] does not work with this system.

questions.q: True or False: weighted prompting works identically across Stable Diffusion, Midjourney/Niji, and Flux.
questions.explain: Weights are most reliable in Stable Diffusion; Flux doesn't support them (or negative prompts), and Midjourney/Niji handles them differently.

questions.q: True or False: you can embed an image directly from your phone or computer's local storage.
questions.explain: Images must be hosted online with a direct link starting with http — you can't reference a local file.

questions.q: What's the purpose of the text inside the brackets, e.g. ![description here]?
options: It's alt text for accessibility / shown if the image fails to load
It's required to match the file name
questions.explain: That's the alt text — used by screen readers and shown if the image link breaks.

questions.q: True or False: if you don't specify a medium, the model defaults to a blended digital style.
questions.explain: Stated directly at the top of Artistic Mediums.

questions.q: Which of these is a "directional lighting" technique in the guide?
questions.explain: Rembrandt lighting (the triangle cheek highlight) is listed under Directional Lighting; the others are texture, environmental, and contrast behaviors respectively.
